When you write `def test(level="WARNING"):`, you get what Clize (and python) calls an optional positional argument. This means you can give it a value just on its own, like this:

    $ python SashaLogbook.py ERROR

When you write `python SashaLogbook.py level=ERROR` your program receives `"level=ERROR"`. None if your `if`s detect it so your program doesn't create a log output. I suggest changing your `if`s to a chain of `if-elif-elif-else`, with an `else` that gives you an error message and exits. That way you can detect if the input is not exactly what you epected.

Now, if you want to be able to write this instead:

    $ python SashaLogbook.py --level=ERROR

Then you need an option parameter:

    def test(*, level="WARNING"):
        ...

It is documented here: http://clize.readthedocs.io/en/stable/basics.html#accepting-options (The page also links to how you can do this on Python 2 -- feel free to ask for details on this too)

This is nice! I have two non-critical suggestions:

* in the child classes like `PaddedFrame`, it's recommended to use `super()` to call the methods in parent class instead of using the parents' name itself.
  * additionally, `unpack()` method with no customization should not be implemented in the child classes like `DataFrame`, it can be implemented by using @property.

```python
# implement property instead of overriding 'unpack()'
@property
def payload(self):
    return self.unpadded_data
```

* the package `binascii` (and `_hexdecode` method) should be written inside `TestFrame` class, as it is used only for testing.

I would like to point out that pyang does infact run validation on the date format (date regex in pyang/syntax.py) in the revision statement.  The above is solely with regards to the file declaration in IETF drafts where the `<CODE BEGINS>` starts and only affects the filename during extraction (before validation)

```
date = r"[1-2][0-9]{3}-(0[1-9]|1[012])-(0[1-9]|[12][0-9]|3[01])"
```

Therefore, the enhancement here would be to solely run validation on the filename itself to make sure that it follows the syntax defined in

https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6020#section-5.2

Stream ciphers use `NONCEBYTES`, whereas AEAD constructions use `NPUBBYTES`. In the CAESAR competition, optional support for a "secret" nonce was specified. Hence the distinction between a "public" and a "secret" nonce. In practice, this specification had little success, because the use cases were pretty limited.
Still, this is something that can easily be added later if needed.

I had a similar error. I'm not an expert but can share what worked for me. The problem is that the corpus does not include all token ids present in the dictionary. If it so happens that the highest-index token in the dictionary is absent from the corpus, the resulting csc matrix will have too few rows, causing the index error. When the csc matrix is created in pyLDAvis.gensim._extract_data, you have to force the number of tokens to equal the length of the dictionary:

corpus_csc = gensim.matutils.corpus2csc(corpus, num_terms=len(fnames_argsort))

(Also shift the definition of fnames_argsort to the beginning of _extract_data)

Fixed in version 1.1.2. Thank you for finding this issue.

No matter what the default Unicode policy is in Python, there's a strict one in JSON.

You can't pretend that JSON can represent arbitrary sequences of bytes:

````
>>> json.dumps({'a': '\xc3'})
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/json/__init__.py", line 243, in dumps
    return _default_encoder.encode(obj)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 207, in encode
    chunks = self.iterencode(o, _one_shot=True)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/json/encoder.py", line 270, in iterencode
    return _iterencode(o, 0)
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf8' codec can't decode byte 0xc3 in position 0: invalid continuation byte
````

If you *do* want to put strings that have defined encoding (binary data) into a JSON object, you *must* first encode it into something that can be encoded as UTF-8 (or UTF-16 or 32).

It turns out sending binary data through mechanisms that can't understand it is an old problem with an old solution:

````
>>> json.dumps({'a_base64': base64.b64encode('\xc3')})
'{"a_base64": "ww=="} 
````

But to get your data back, you have to know which fields were base64 encoded.  Unfortunately you can't really do this with as simple a recursive function as you linked;  you need something [a little more complicated](https://developers.google.com/discovery/v1/type-format).

By now this comment probably reads a lot Armin Ronacher's "Unicode in Python 3" blogposts, which you mentioned.  That's not a coincidence!  It's the same problem but with JSON instead of Python 3.   And implicit decoding of binary data as it crosses systems boundaries, which this issue would add, would make the situation *more* like what Armin complained about.  That's the *real* problem addressed [here](http://lucumr.pocoo.org/2014/5/12/everything-about-unicode/) -- Python 3 attempts to guess the encoding of, say, stdin when there may not even be one!  

It's best to not commit the same error again, but with JSON.

Interesting question, I reckon it wouldn't be called django-_front_ any longer? :)

Django-front doesn't actually support languages, i.e. it's only a matter of the context you pass to the template tag, which *could* be a LANGUAGE_CODE but could also be something entirely other. And the actual placeholder doesn't keep track of the passed arguments, just the hash of the sum of the arguments. So you couldn't say give me all the placeholder content bits for e.g. English.

All in all I would say no, as it defeats the purpose of front-end editing, and what you're trying to do is provided by more apter solutions such as e.g. django-cms

For these large values, are you decrypting and saving them to disk?

Currently asn1crypto was developed dealing with files that fit in memory. Most of the structures are being used in some form or another for X.509 certificates, CRLs, OCSP or PKCS#12 files.

I am thinking that for this to work, there would need to be a way to pass a file object to `Asn1Value.load()` instead of a byte string. Then, each object would copy any necessary header and footer bytes, but the contents would be a reference to the file object, plus an offset and length. That way the file contents would never need to be copied into memory for large chunks of data.

For serializing object via `.dump()`, there would need to be an optional file object parameter that would accept all of the output.

While the library can parse indefinite length values, I think the real challenge will be how to restructure the existing functionality to treat the `.contents` attribute of each `Asn1Value` object as something other than just a byte string.

and `quoted_word` looks like this:
```
def quoted_word(view):
    current = view.current

    if current is None:
        return None

    is_quoted = current in quotes.keys()
    if is_quoted:
        open_quote = current
        close_quote = quotes[open_quote]
    else:
        open_quote = close_quote = '"'

    result = [] if is_quoted else [current]

    while not view.eof:
        current = view.get()
        if not current:
            if is_quoted:
                # unexpected EOF
                raise BadArgument('Expected closing {}'.format(close_quote))
            return ''.join(result)

        # currently we accept strings in the format of "hello world"
        # to embed a quote inside the string you must escape it: "a \"world\""
        if current == '\\':
            next_char = view.get()
            if not next_char:
                # string ends with \ and no character after it
                if is_quoted:
                    # if we're quoted then we're expecting a closing quote
                    raise BadArgument(
                        'Expected closing {}'.format(close_quote))
                # if we aren't then we just let it through
                return ''.join(result)

            if next_char in (open_quote, close_quote):
                # escaped quote
                result.append(next_char)
            else:
                # different escape character, ignore it
                view.undo()
                result.append(current)
            continue

        # closing quote
        if current == close_quote:
            next_char = view.get()
            valid_eof = not next_char or next_char.isspace()
            if is_quoted:
                if not valid_eof:
                    raise BadArgument('Expected space after closing quotation')

                # we're quoted so it's okay
                return ''.join(result)
            else:
                # we aren't quoted
                raise BadArgument('Unexpected quote mark in non-quoted string')

        if current.isspace() and not is_quoted:
            # end of word found
            return ''.join(result)

        result.append(current)
```

I some cases, you can't manage the renewal logic in cron. For instance, if you have more than five certificates for a domain, your script cannot ensure that all of them will be renewed (in fact, I'm pretty sure it will always renew the five same certs, and leave the rest expire). By checking the expiration date of all the certs every day, and not only launching the script every 30 days, you can avoid this problem.

This is the same reason why I don't stop if a renewal fails. A renewal can fail for many reasons (quota expired, nginx misconfigured for this vhost, etc…), but the renewal of the next cert could be completely fine. I don't see any reason to stop on a failure.

This seems to be because Python treats empty things as *falseish*.

@tgarc You should try it on a non-empty file, and you @bastibe on an empty one.

> Is this intended behavior?

I guess up to your discovery it was accidental behavior.

> builtin file objects evaluate to `True`

That's true, even for empty files. Because they don't support `len()` ...

> Would you be alright with adding a `__bool__` method to return `True`?

I don't think that's the right solution.

I think the problem is that we provide `__len__()` for something that's not an actual sequence.
We should remove `__len__()` and replace it by something like `SoundFile.frames`, analogous to the `SoundFile.channels` we already have.

Of course this would break backwards compatibility, so we should think about if it is worth it and at least provide a migration path.

The `closefd` argument is not supposed to have an effect if you use file-like objects.

It only comes into action if you use *file descriptors*. In Python you can get them for example with `os.open()`, but probably also from some external libraries.
You can get the underlying file descriptor of a Python file object with the `fileno()` method, but I don't know what happens to the file object if you close its file descriptor ...

BTW, this functionality is not implemented in Python, it is a feature of the libsndfile library itself.

The situation I ran into is actually the second one you mentioned, where the document does not terminate with a newline. I was writing integration tests for an application and using short strings (without newlines) as the test input data. Though it was not my intention, I do believe this is still a realistic scenario; the Commonmark spec section 2.1 does not seem to require a terminal newline, and Markdown can be used in web apps that store data in, say, a database instead of a file.

Even when the document is being read from a file, I don't believe it's safe to assume a newline. POSIX requires one, but Windows (and probably other systems) does not, and of course it would be nice to accommodate non-compliant files on POSIX systems if it's not difficult.

Perhaps the best solution is to simply append a newline in `Document.__init__` if one is not present? Then the footnote parser can remain unchanged with the assumption that there is at least one newline

@mjdavies cron will automatically purge all environment variables when it runs, so if your app depends on any of those (e.g. you're using a virtual environment, or rely on some environment variables), the jobs will fail to run (that's a common issue running `cron` in containers). 

Unfortunately, since `cron` also eats you log output, this can be a little tough to debug. One way to try and work around that is to send logs from your task somewhere else to get an idea of what's going wrong.

For example, alter the `crontab` generated by `django-crontab` like this, then run `cron -f`: 

```
*/5 * * * * /usr/bin/python /code/manage.py crontab run d5fc228456872d633a9fd2f18a2ace15 >> /tmp/debug.log 2>&1
```

Afterwards, wait for a little while (i.e. 5 minutes until the job runs) and check `/tmp/debug.log` to get an idea of what went wrong.

---

Now... I apologize for barging in like this in this thread, but I wrote an open-source crontab job runner that's designed to solve this particular pain point of runnung `cron` in containers, so I figured you might want to give it a shot! It's over here: https://github.com/aptible/supercronic

To use it with django-crontab, all you need to do is generate the `crontab` with `django-crontab`, then point `supercronic` at the `crontab` that was generated by `django-crontab` (instead of running `cron -f`).

(@kraiz - do feel free to remove the Supercronic tip if you think this kind of promotion isn't appropriate!)

After rethinking about the problem, this patch seems to fix the described issue about my PR #40. However, there's a problem with the fix of #18:

There may be a fourth submodule `D` inside `B` and `B` doesn't want to pack `externals/D/foo.txt`. `git archive-all` does indeed check, if it should be ignored, however there's a problem with the paths. These will be checked in the following order:
1. Inside `D`, `foo.txt` will be checked against `D`'s `.gitignore`. This works just fine.
2. Inside `B`, `foo.txt` will be checked against `B`'s `.gitignore`. However, the path to be checked is *NOT* `externals/D/foo.txt` but the full path inside `A`, i.e. `externals/B/externals/D/foo.txt`. This needs to be fixed.
3. Inside `A`, `foo.txt` will be checked against `A`'s `.gitignore` with the full path `externals/B/externals/D/foo.txt`. This works just fine.

@mozillazg  报错是必然的, 是因为pypinyin\utils.py", line 48, in simple_seg这个函数报的错`must be unicode string or [unicode, ...] list`
原因就是因为在IronPython运行runtime内, 读取到的Version是2.7.7  而我装的python是2.7.13  这说明了IronPython并不是使用的Python的相关版本之类的信息, compat.py文件的`PY2 = sys.version_info < (3, 0)`这行代码检测到是2.7.7版本,便使得
```
text_type = unicode
bytes_type = str
```
但有IronPython有个坑爹的地方
IronPython运行环境执行如下:
```
C:\Program Files (x86)\IronPython 2.7>ipy
IronPython 2.7.7 (2.7.7.0) on .NET 4.0.30319.42000 (32-bit)
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> unicode
<type 'str'>
>>> str
<type 'str'>
>>> isinstance(unicode(u'成都'), str)
True
```
上面的代码是IronPython在命令行中运行的结果,  也就是说, 在IronPython环境下, unicode就是str  str也是str
所以在utils文件的simple_seg函数以下代码**绝对会报错**
```
assert not isinstance(hans, bytes_type), \
    'must be unicode string or [unicode, ...] list'
```
因为bytes_type在上面PY2判断时设置了为str, 这就悲剧了, 因为isinstance(<type 'unicode'>, str) 是True

**因为IronPython要和C#交互, 所以在IronPython中的str就是unicode, 所以`unicode`恒等于`str`**
---
然后我在你的代码上更改了`compat.py`文件的`PY2`变量的判断, 就可以正常运行了:
```
PY2 = sys.version_info < (3, 0) and sys.subversion[0]!='IronPython' # and 后面是我添加的,只加了这个判断
if not PY2:
    text_type = str
    bytes_type = bytes
else:
    text_type = unicode           # noqa
    bytes_type = str
```

Here's a minimal test program. It's interesting because it shows that the issues with the CIGAR aren't just random insertions/deletions.

```python
import parasail
import tempfile

MATRIX_TEMPLATE = \
"""# Auto-generated substitution matrix
       A        C        G        T    N
A  %(m)d   %(mm)d   %(mm)d   %(mm)d    0
C  %(mm)d  %(m)d   %(mm)d   %(mm)d    0
G  %(mm)d  %(mm)d   %(m)d   %(mm)d    0
T  %(mm)d  %(mm)d   %(mm)d   %(m)d    0
N       0       0        0       0    0
"""

def make_matrix(match, mismatch):
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile() as tf:
        tf.write((MATRIX_TEMPLATE % {'mm': mismatch, 'm': match}).encode('ascii'))
        tf.flush()
        return parasail.Matrix(tf.name)

matrix = make_matrix(1, -4)
seq = b'N'
ref_seq = b'A'

result = parasail.nw_trace(seq, ref_seq, 6, 1, matrix)
assert result.cigar.decode == b'1X', result.cigar.decode
assert result.score == 0, result.score
```

should be able to not need it.

sudo cp config/generated/nginx.conf /etc/nginx/sites-enabled
This means that you can't run two instances of CodaLab very easily on the
same machine without opening up the hood.

Just as virtualenv nicely allows non-root users to maintain different
Python environment, I think people should be able to try out different
CodaLab versions/environments in the same way.

Hello,

Thanks for the compliment.

The parser is simply a flask-restful parser.
It's mainly used to extract simple attributes from the query strings, the headers or the request payload (form, file or json).

Models (and fields) purpose is to describe complex structure extracted from the request payload (it allows nested, list...).

You can mix them depending on your needs.

My personal use case, is to use the request parser to validate query parameters, headers and file upload and to use models coupled with ``@api.marshal_with()``, ``@api.expect()`` and ``@api.response()`` to describe payloads (expected and returned).

My APIs expect and respond JSON in their payloads (with the file upload/download exceptions), but given your gist, you seems to use formdata.
I forked and modified your gist: https://gist.github.com/noirbizarre/fe30e4578f312853638f

- only specify a model when you return (40X responses does not return the expected model)
- ``@api.marshal_with`` decorator document your response, so only use ``@api.response`` for other cases (errors)
- try to return the object and a status code (if different from the default 200) instead of the verbatim for the status code

If you want to be more restful (I know, this is a buzz word), try to use JSON as input instead of formdata.

Payload validation for model will come in next release with [jsonschema](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/jsonschema).

If you literally want to pass extra data to `context` for use in the template, you can subclass the `RegistrationView` you are using and add the appropriate data in `get_context_data`.

For example:

```python
class CustomRegistrationView(RegistrationView):
    def get_context_data(self, **kwargs):
        context = super(RegistrationView, self).get_context_data(**kwargs)
        # add your additional data here
        context['foo'] = 'bar'
        return context
```

I had had in my head that the intention was that neither package require each other, but looking over the code it is clear that languages_plus does require countries_plus in order to provide the countries_spoken field. There are two ways to go to resolve this.

**Properly drop the requirement**
Without breaking compatibility with existing installations, I suppose a fix would be to add a countries_spoken_raw field that is a comma separated list of country iso codes, and add a countries_spoken @property that would use the countries_spoken_raw field to do a lookup on the Country table (if installed) and return the results.  That way countries_spoken would still work for people relying on it.  However, that would still cause issues with anyone using the reverse relationship (which would no longer exist).

**Add the requirement**
 I think the most sensible thing to do here is just to add countries_plus as an explicit requirement.  Particularly because presumably everyone using it so far must also be using countries_plus because of that implicit requirement, so the additional requirement shouldn't impact anyone.  

Any thoughts on these solutions?

@gsalgado I don't think @cburgdorf and I are necessarily on the same page with this (which isn't necessarily a bad thing).  If our two ideas don't sound congruent that may be because they are not.

@cburgdorf your idea about `--kovan` is probably orthogonal to whether syncing is done as a plugin or not.

First, converting sync to be a plugin is probably very low priority, at least until we get the plugin architecture better figured out.

Second, reasoning behind wanting it to be done via the plugin system (if it turns out to be reasonable to do so) is fueled by a few things:

- dogfooding: ideally, if it fits nicely in the plugin system it eventually goes there.
- flexibility: if the *plugin* platform on trinity catches on, we could see 3rd party syncing approaches get developed.
- modularity: plugins should be well isolated, only consuming well documented public APIs.  This will in theory result in code that allows us to swap new theoretical implementations of the syncing approaches.

Another *way* to look at this might be to consider sharding and the beacon chain.  There will be more than a single chain to sync at some point here, and being able to do all of those in a modular way sounds nice.

I'm feeling like it's going to be better to address this in `twisted.web` than in Klein, especially since we're in the same dev org and whatnot.

I see two options.  One is to add get/set methods as you suggest.  I think that should also deprecate `request.args`, as that becomes overlapping functionality that allows you to so something bad like set non-`bytes` values.  Since that's probably highly-used API, that'll be a bit of a bother.

Note that I really dislike `get` as a prefix on accessors, so I'd use `request.argument(name)` and `request.setArgument(name, value)` as the API.  The [Twisted Coding Standard](http://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/core/development/policy/coding-standard.html) is silent on this issue, unfortunately, and Twisted uses the `get` prefix intermittently.  Perhaps others have more info on that.

The other option is to turn `request.args` into a property and have it give you an `Arguments` instead of a `dict`.  I'm not sure if that fits into the [Twisted Compatibility Policy](https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/core/development/policy/compatibility-policy.html), but we'll probably want `Arguments` to be as `dict`-like as possible.

No, you misundertand what I mean is: 
- upconvert on PyTRiP user level all values to float32, no matter what the source originally was int16 or something completely different. Then we can handle any mulitplication whatsoever while user is working with pytrip.
- when saving, things will down-converted to whatever TRiP98 usually uses for their respective files.

Again, think into real use cases, and what should be saved is different than what is calculated.
This is reasonable, since actual dose cubes which TRiP98 handles should not contain "insane values" anyway. The overflows we see are only of temporary nature, when doing various arithmetic, however the cubes saved, will be "nice" in most cases.

Then of course there may be a few cases (< 10 %) when cubes wont be nice, and these files will most likely never (< 1 %) ever be read by TRiP98. 
Here, the write() functions must fall-back to save the full float32 and print a warning on write() level.

Theoretically, TC's visualization tool should allow you to plot the ROC by using the `tc.show()` function:
```
turicreate.show(ev['roc_curve']['fpr'], ev['roc_curve']['tpr'])
```  
However, as mentioned in https://github.com/apple/turicreate/issues/142, it's not yet possible to request a specific plot type (a scatter plot in this case) - so sadly the plot won't show correctly for the moment.  

The quickest workaround is to use `matplotlib`:
```
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.scatter(ev['roc_curve']['fpr'], ev['roc_curve']['tpr'])
```
If you're using Jupyter notebooks, you can add `%matplotlib inline` after importing `matplotlib`, this will make the drawing appear within the notebook.

Had to fix this for SR830 in T3 drivers. Did that by overwriting snapshot_base to skip array parameters and conductance. Conductance can only be measured if channel1 is set to measure X (not R or ...) we cannot enforce that to be the case in snapshot so exclude it

```
    def snapshot_base(self, update=False, params_to_skip_update=None):
        if params_to_skip_update is None:
            params_to_skip_update = ('conductance', 'ch1_databuffer', 'ch2_databuffer')
        snap = super().snapshot_base(update=update,
                                     params_to_skip_update=params_to_skip_update)
        return snap
```

This is now ready for reviewing. The following traitlets now support bounds checking:

 - Int and CInt
 - Integer
 - Long and CLong
 - Float and CFloat

In C{Int,Long}, the max bounds check may be a little surprising:

```python
class SomeClass(HasTraits):
    trait = CInt(5, max=10)

a = SomeClass()
a.trait = 10.5 # does not raise
a.trait == 10 # true
```

I think this is the right behaviour, since the *validated* value of `a` is within bounds, but I'm happy to implement a different behaviour if people don't think this is correct.

Sorry for the delay in response. Okay first off @gamaanderson can you tell me which is the "old" and "new"? The first plot above looks more like the airborne radar I would expect.

To comment on the structure of the program. There is some back story (as always) with airborne radar data. Before about 2012, the data were saved differently, in blocks of code containing ray data for a block of time. A lot of hardware changes were made at that time and after much discussion, we decided that saving one *sweep* might be the best way forward. However, many legacy programs deal with data on a time period basis. This mean that a number of sweeps - or more properly a continuously rotating antenna would form a sort of conical volume. Remember that this scanning is intrinsically different than ground-based radar as the platform is moving at non-negligilbe speed. New methods were not (and I would argue still ARE not developed) and these data *look* different than you would expect from a nice stationary ground system. 

I have not dove back in to the data recently. But will try to speak with some people soon about this.
The data go through more geolocation steps due to movement. In addition, aircraft sampling angles constantly change (turns, turbulence, etc.). So the data turns out to be more complicated than the average radar in geometric terms.

IF plans continue as expected a second antenna will be added to this type of radar system. Other airborne platforms (not active scanners) already have up to 5 potential antennas. This complicates matters well beyond anything PyART can currently handle and I don't have a clue at the moment how to handle this. So the airborne class may become outdated.

Hello @XuelinZeng and @ilaxes,

Thank you for your post. The difference between [getWordVector](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/master/src/fasttext.cc#L64) and [getSentenceVector](https://github.com/facebookresearch/fastText/blob/master/src/fasttext.cc#L428) is that the latter used getWordVector to assemble a single vector for a sequence of tokens (words).

That means, you use getWordVector if you want to receive an embeddings for a single word and getSentenceVector if you want to get an embedding for a sentence (sequence of words).

For a supervised model getSentenceVector will simply average the word vectors for each word in a line of text. For all other models (cbow and skipgram) getSentenceVector will divide each word vector by it's norm and then average them. Now, it is important to keep in mind that any sentence will end with a newline. That means "one two" actually translates into the vectors for "one", "two" and EOS.

```
$ ./fasttext supervised -input dbpedia.train -output model -thread 10 -epoch 8 -verbose 2 -dim 2
Read 32M words
Number of words:  803537
Number of labels: 14
Progress: 100.0% words/sec/thread: 3130504 lr:  0.000000 loss:  0.507742 ETA:   0h 0m
```

```
$ ./fasttext print-sentence-vectors asdf.bin
one two
2.6772 -3.0886
```

```
$ ./fasttext print-word-vectors asdf.bin
one
one -0.0065439 0.1416
two
two 0.086216 0.10804
</s>
</s> 7.9518 -9.5155
```

We now have (-0.0065439 + 0.086216 + 7.9518) / 3 = 2.6772 and (0.1416 + 0.10804 + -9.5155) / 3 = -3.0886 as expected for a supervised model.

I'm closing this issue now as I consider it resolved, but please feel encouraged to reopen it at any time if you don't. 

Thanks,
Christian

> I think anything that special-cases mocked objects sorta defeats the point

I was thinking the same thing.

The use case for custom `before` and `after` specification is reasonable I suppose. That said, I don't *love* the idea of adding in a wrapper/callback here. I would probably prefer to refactor `rrulebase` such that it's easier to do this sort of thing (and possibly more) with inheritance.

One thing I will note is that I did run into just this kind of issue some time ago when I was looking at [`dateutil.tz.tzical`](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/tz/tz.py#L1035), which is time zone specifications using the iCal RFC spec (the same RFC that defines RRULEs). IIRC, I wanted to be able to attach some metadata to each date to prevent a lot of the hoop-jumping that goes on there. I think any "event wrapper" refactoring should take that use case into account.

I'll have to give this more thought, but two options leap out at me:

1. Add a public `date_wrapper` member function to `rrulebase` that can be overridden in child classes.
2. Replace `date` constructors with a reference to `self.Date = date`, or `self.DateTime = datetime`, so that the `Date` and `DateTime` objects can be replaced with custom classes.

I'm immediately thinking that the first option is much better than the second. That said, the problem with this is that you really are still only able to pass a function that takes a date and transforms it (presumably deterministically) into an event with no possibility of passing other metadata, it's not clear how any interface that takes additional metadata would look though.

@abalkin I haven't in any way foreclosed the idea of reconsidering #264. The need to compromise in this way is unfortunate.

In the end, my plan is to have the `tzinfo` object fall back to the `datetime`'s `fold` attribute whenever possible. I don't think the current implementation does that quite correctly, but given that there is no final PEP-495 implementation yet I figured I would re-assess the situation when I had something stable to build against.

Regarding 
```python
>>> t0 + timedelta(hours=1) == t1
False
```
I don't understand what you're trying to say with this. From my understanding of datetime arithmetic, this is the *expected* behavior - datetimes are a 7-handed clock and timedeltas are intended to move the hands of the clock around. 2 AM is a different time than second-1AM.

>In any case, assuming a backport of PEP 495 or Python 3.6 datetime module is available, how should dateutil use it? Would you really want to change program behavior depending on whether PEP 495 implementation is available?

My philosophy on this is that it's OK for the behavior to be a little weird when using a non-PEP 495 `datetime` as long as it works as expected when PEP 495 is implemented. The whole point of PEP 495 is to fix these sorts of ambiguities, so it's not unreasonable to say that if you're not using a version with PEP-495 implemented, you're going to have ambiguous behavior. The reason I'd like a `datetime` backport is that I could then assume all users have the *option* to use the consistent behavior if they care about it. I'm willing to be convinced otherwise, though.

A `datetime` backport would also solve another issue that I've been grappling with, which is that even post- #264, there's no simple way to construct an ambiguous `datetime` other than by converting it from another timezone-aware datetime. `pytz` has a distinct advantage here because you can specify the dst behavior in the `localize` function.

It seems to me that there are three ways to get around this:

1. expose the "dst preference" on the `tzinfo` object,
2. use a localizer-function like `pytz` is doing or
3. use a `datetime` that has the `fold` attribute. If I knew a backport of `datetime` were available, I could say to end users, "If you want consistent behavior around DST, and if you want to be able to construct ambiguous datetimes, use 3.6 or use the backport", then `dateutil` doesn't have to muck around with some hacked-together interface for generating ambiguous datetimes.

My quick take on this, after some discussions with @ellisonbg and @Carreau:

- I personally hadn't actually noticed that the left blue borders in master weren't the same colors we had discussed in our "D modified" discussion in #708. They are similar enough that, until Brian pointed that fact out, I had to go and measure the RGB values to actually confirm they weren't the same, b/c on quick glance they looked close enough.

- Re. the green border, I personally actually *like* the current, all-around green border.  I have this week been using the notebook 'in anger' in writing long and complex notebooks, with lots of back-and-forth between command and edit mode, and I actually found the full green highlight quite useful in marking the state of being in command mode to me, without being so garish as to be annoying.   Note that I'm stating *my* opinion here as a data point from one user, not making a decision.  It's just that we need to converge quickly on a decision so we can wrap it up, so for now it will be up to our own sense of balance between the usefulness of conveying that information with the green highlight and not going overboard.

- Re the thin blue border on selected cells: it feels a bit inconsistent to me in that it's only on the right and not on the top/bottom, and thus it's different from every other visual element we already have (the other outlines are all complete).  And it's true that it was never part of our discussions in #708.  Now, it's not an end-of-the-world issue, but I agree that it's not great to have there, I just hadn't noticed it before.

So the question at this point is, do we hold a release over this or not? They are obviously UI issues and not core functionality bugs, but the reality is that we also have #893 that will require extra work, so I suggest that we go ahead and polish this up since we can, while that nasty problem gets sorted out...  

If we were literally about to pull the trigger on a release and it came down only to these issues, we might think differently about it, but given that making these changes is probably a tiny PR with:

- Removal of the little blue right-border.
- [Optionally] change the blue shades to the ones from the #708 discussion.

That's probably very doable.

We can then ask on-list about the green outline for cmd mode.  As I said, I'd be personally quite happy leaving it as-is as I like this version. But others might disagree, and if consensus is strongly in the opposite direction, I have no problem with that.

How does this sound?  I suspect that #893 will give us plenty of time to do the above before cutting an actual release anyway...

It might not be hard to do data collection and upload without a server, by having the metrics collection library (a) store data directly using sqlite or something that has a strategy for coordinating multiple independent readers/writers, and (b) notice when it's time to do an upload and spawn off a little background process to do it and reset the database when it succeeds. I guess we'll need most of this anyway given that we want our metrics to still work even if someone is running multiple notebook servers at the same time in the same home directory. But we definitely need help from the server for consent, so for version 0.1 we might as well do whatever's simplest.

While it's true that there are a lot of limitations to count data, I think there are some compelling reasons to still want it:

* Relative comparisons over time: even if it's meaningless to see that there are 2 million calls to `foo` in March, it's potentially meaningful to see that in April this has dropped to 100,000.
* Relative comparisons between features: there's some debate in NumPy right now about if, supposing we change how `np.dot` works in some corner cases, whether we should also change the public C API function that currently it just calls under the hood (`PyArray_MatrixProduct2`). For discussing this, it would be useful to know how many people are using the C API directly versus the Python-level API -- but every call to the Python-level API also causes a call to the C API. One way to solve this would be to count calls to both separately, and then the difference is the number of direct calls to the C API.

I'd suggest making the data collection API look like:

```
metrics.add(project_key, feature, value=None, count=1)
metrics.register_pull_function(pull_function)
```

Example:

metrics.add("numpy-5605-4de1-ae10", "version", "1.9.1")

def numpy_pull_stats():
    # For speed, `PyArray_MatrixProduct` counts how many times it is called in a C-level variable
    # which we batch up and submit to the metrics engine later
    pycount, ccount = get_stats_from_c_variables()
    stats.submit("numpy-5605-4de1-ae10", "dot.python-level", count=pycount)
    stats.submit("numpy-5605-4de1-ae10", "dot.c-level", count=ccount)

Idea:

`project_key` is a unique string identifying the group who is taking responsibility for collecting this data. The idea being that if you want to get your data back out of ipython's server, then you have to register one of these keys and affirm that you've read our privacy doc and thought about what data you'll collect.

`feature` is an arbitrary (unicode) string identifying what thing you are counting (scoped by `project_key`), by convention hierarchical by dots, b/c conventions are nice.

`value` is another arbitrary (unicode) string, or None. We count (feature, value) pairs. The split between feature and value is arbitrary (one could just encode the (feature, value) tuple as a single string), but IME in these kind of little metric systems key/value pairs are so common that it's nice to have sugar for them. An alternative would be to allow `value` to be an arbitrary JSON-encodable value.

Firefox has some interesting documents to potentially use as a model:

https://www.mozilla.org/en-US/privacy/principles/
https://www.mozilla.org/en-US/privacy/firefox/

Ok, interesting. @rgbkrk hinted at this the other day but I didn't quite understand the issue... 

Some high-level questions:

* What is the rationale for allowing an output to have multiple mime types?
* What is the rationale for isolating mime types?
* What are the dangers of allowing a renderer to access data/metadata of other mime types?

Some things to consider:

* Jupyterlab has supported mime bundle _injection_ for a while and @blink1073 recently refactored the rendermime interface to make output data and metadata observable and editable by a renderer: https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/pull/1709
* According to @ellisonbg, including a static image in the mime bundle is a requirement for jupyterlab_vega (as ipyvega provided this and it's proven to be an important feature): https://github.com/altair-viz/jupyterlab_vega/issues/4

My thoughts:

A renderer is responsible for rendering output of a specific mime type in the notebook. The notebook is no longer a single environment but many (classic notebook, jupyterlab, nteract, nbviewer, Github's nbviewer, etc.). Ideally, a renderer will work across environments, but it doesn't. As a result, instead of seeing a nice plot on Github, you end up seeing `<altair.Vega object>`. How can this be improved? Well, all notebook environments can render `image/png` and `text/html`. The renderer on the client-side is well-suited to provide `image/png` and `text/html` representations of data that's already been renderered vs. a library of the kernel-side. Additionally, if the renderer is responsible for providing fallback mime types, then all kernels benefit from this feature, not just IPython. Now, it's not perfect (I'm seeing many edge cases where both static images and HTML fall short, especially on Github), but it solves a legitimate problem. 

In my ideal world, a user would not be required to install an extension for every mime type they would like to render because this adds a lot of complexity to a user's workflow and makes reproducibility less guaranteed. I did [a little experiment](https://github.com/gnestor/jupyterlab_react) that lazy loads remote renderers, meaning that as soon as an output is available, the renderer fetches the required package for that mime type from npm, dynamically imports it using SystemJS, and renders it using using that package (which is a React component). Now don't get me wrong, this raises **huge** security concerns, but it demonstrates how renderers could be more dynamic and less coupled with the environment.

We only do that particular bit of logging on resources, not clients, so even if we did add a log sort feature it wouldn't be very broad. However, you can do your own logging using the event system. This is better because you can do whatever you want, and it can apply to every call made in both resources and clients. Here's an example:

```python
import json
import logging

import boto3

LOG = logging.getLogger(__name__)


def log_sorted_args(params, **kwargs):
    log_message = 'SORTED PARAMS: %s' % json.dumps(params, sort_keys=True)
    LOG.debug(log_message)


boto3.set_stream_logger('')
session = boto3.Session()
session.events.register('provide-client-params.*.*', log_sorted_args)

s3 = session.client('s3')
s3.list_objects_v2(Bucket='my-bucket', Delimiter='/')


# You can also add it to the default session
if boto3.DEFAULT_SESSION is None:
    boto3.setup_default_session()
session = boto3.DEFAULT_SESSION
session.events.register('provide-client-params.*.*', log_sorted_args)

s3 = boto3.client('s3')
s3.list_objects_v2(Bucket='my-bucket', Delimiter='/')


# Or you can add it to individual clients / resources
client = boto3.client('s3')
client.meta.events.register('provide-client-params.*.*', log_sorted_args)
resource = boto3.resource('s3')
resource.meta.client.meta.events.register(
    'provide-client-params.*.*', log_sorted_args)
```

Whenever you make a call you would see something like this:

```
2017-10-26 09:50:56,236 __main__ [DEBUG] SORTED PARAMS: {"Bucket": "my-bucket", "Delimiter": "/"}
```

How do you imagine the local override to work for nested structures like Environment Variables. There are two options. Let me illustrate:

Assume this is the template (obvious properties omitted for simplicity):

```
FunctionTemplate:
  Type: AWS::Serverless::Template
  Properties:
    Environment:
      Variables:
         TableName: foo

MyFirstFunction:
  Properties:
    Template: !Ref FunctionTemplate
    Environment:
       Variables:
          DBName: bar

MySecondFunction:
  Properties:
    Template: !Ref FunctionTemplate
```

There are two options:

**1. Environment variables get merged:**: So, `MyFirstFunction` will get both `TableName` and `DBName` variable

**2. Environment variables get overwritten**: `MyFirstFunction` gets only `DBName` because you have overridden the `Environment` property entirely.

Option 1 is weird because it literally merges two different dictionaries which can cause complications. It will be even weird for `Events` section which can have a multi-level dictionary and even arrays.

Option 2 is easier to understand, easy to reason about and gives most of the benefits. 

What do you guys prefer? What do you want to use in your templates?

Rebased and completed the last batch of changes (last 3 commits). I think that should be it.

@aldanor You can see the latest changes to `array_t` which I isolated in the last commit. This makes its converting ctor behave the same as other pytypes, i.e. throws on error with the original Python message. The `ensure` function is intended for `type_caster` and it silently discards the error. The special nature of `array_t` now boils down to 3 things:

1. It needs its own `type_caster` which uses `ensure` (as opposed to a simple check like the other pytypes). It will need to stay this way.
2. `py::isinstance<array_t<T>>()` is not supported. This can probably be resolved, but there is no quick solution in the numpy C API, so a something custom would need to be made.
3. It doesn't have a meaningful default constructor like other concrete pytypes. Right now it's just `nullptr`.

The last two points can be changed, but that's probably best done as part of the `array_t` rework -- I don't interfere with that any further.

One thing that #916 exposes is that a `maybe<T>` needs to have a dependency for casters like `Eigen::Ref`.  More details in #916, but the short version is that the Eigen::Ref caster sometimes keeps a temporary `py::array` alive via a caster member when a temporary copy is needed, but the `stl.h` casters don't keep the subcasters alive, so we end up with an Eigen::Ref that references the data of a destroyed `py::array`.

So, here are ideas:
* Add a new option, `-DPYBIND_USE_COMPILER_FEATURES`, that toggles the compiler features behavior. I'm not fond of adding extra settings, but this may be the least invasive.
* Simply change the compiler feature request to match the old auto-choice. I'll need to see if it can be downgraded by a project. If so, this might be the best option.
* Add a `pybind11::module11` target that is always C++11, and let the normal target and the `add_` command be the latest possible

Even in the current implementation, nothing is changed if you use `PYBIND_CPP_STANDARD`.

Thoughts?

The null values exist because msrest deserializes the response into the strongly typed object. Since the values aren't included in the response, they end up as null values in the resulting object. We can handle this with a transform of the output.

I definitely don't recommend two flags.  However, you can have a three state solution that works. @shhsu and I discussed the following:
**create**
```
--admin-enabled  (True is implied)
--admin-enabled True (explicit)
--admin-enabled False (explicit)
(omitting is implicitly false)
```

**update**
```
--admin-enabled True (explicit)
--admin-enabled False (explicit)
(omitting means don't change)
```

In this way, you have consistency between create and update for those who value that, you have the ease of simply specifying a flag on create for those who value that, and you don't have a breaking change.

On Windows, the benefits are:
* paths are case-insensitive
* `adlfs.core.glob` is simpler and avoids double-escaping backslash issues when creating glob pattern
* remote paths can be defined using either backslashes or forward slashes (Azure handles this already but now the library also does)
* if we switch to pathlib for local paths, we can eliminate `glob` and `os.path` imports and have consistent functionality, including support for `**` on all versions.

On all systems, the benefits are:
* users can pass both strings and Path/PurePath objects (similar to PEP 519, 'Path-like' objects)
* syntactic sugar for path concatenation using division operator

I also spent some time trying to figure out if there was a way to making blocking I/O cancellable. I don't think there is.

The first idea I considered is: start a thread that will sit blocked in `read` or `write`, and then if we want to cancel it, use `pthread_kill` to send a signal to trigger an EINTR. The problem is that this is inherently racy for the same reason that [pthread cancellation requires special kernel help](https://lwn.net/Articles/683118/) – you might have the signal arrive just before entering the syscall, or it might arrive just after successfully exiting, and you want to treat these differently (in the first case return failure, in the second case return successfully), but there's absolutely no way to tell the difference between them except by examining the instruction pointer, which requires you write your own asm for doing syscalls. So that's out.

The second idea I considered is: `dup` the fd, issue a `read` or `write` on the dup, and then if you want to cancel the `read` or `write` early, close the dup. (We can't close the original, because we still need it, but we can close the dup.) Unfortunately, on Linux at least this doesn't work: `read` on a pipe doesn't actually return until the *write* side of the pipe has been fully closed (i.e., there are no remaining fds pointing to it). If the fd it's actually using disappears out from under it then oh well, it doesn't care. ...And even if this worked, there'd still be a race condition if we closed the fd just before entering `read`, because it could be re-opened by another thread in the mean time. I guess we could fix the race condition by `dup2`ing a bad fd on top of the original fd, but that still doesn't help with the part where you can't wake it up.

OH WAIT THOUGH. What if we combine these. Option 3: `dup` the fd. Dispatch a blocking `read` or `write` to a thread using the dup. On cancellation, use `dup2` to atomically overwrite the original fd with one for which we know `read`/`write` will fail (e.g. an unconnected socket). Then use `pthread_kill` to send a no-op signal to the worker thread.

If the `dup2` happens before we enter `read`/`write`, then they'll fail immediately, like we want.

Otherwise, it means the `dup2` happens after we enter `read`/`write`, which implies that the signal does as well. So one possibility is that the signal arrives while we're still in `read`/`write`. In this case it returns early with EINTR, CPython attempts to re-issue the call, and then the new calls fails with EBADF because this is after the `dup2`. Alternatively, the signal arrives after the `read`/`write` have completed, in which case it does nothing, which is again what we want.

This still has the problems that we have to claim a signal, and if we're running outside the main thread then Python doesn't provide an API for registering a signal handler (and I'm pretty sure that to get EINTR we need to have a C-level signal handler registered, even though we want it to just be a no-op). But we could potentially grab, like, SIGURG which *hopefully* no-one actually uses and is ignored by default, and use ctypes to call `sigaction`.

This is kind of a terrible idea, but I do think it would work reliably and portably on all Unixes for all fd types.

Python already keeps track of all live threads via `list(threading.enumerate())` (as well as of all live interpreters if you're using CPython's subinterpreter support), so requesting a comparable level of runtime accounting for native coroutines seems reasonable. Independently of your use case here, such a mechanism would be useful for doing system state dumps in tools like `faulthandler`, since it would make it straightforward to dump suspended coroutines in addition to running ones.

I've had a realization that the original goal of this ticket is actually trivially accomplishable using a mechanism similar to `@console_rule`: basically, have v1 `Tasks` declare a `v2` product type that they need computed for `TargetRoots` (using precisely the same mechanism as `@console_rule`). You'd then install an `@rule` to handle computing that product type pre-fork.

The difference from what I originally suggested here is pretty slim: it's essentially just that rather than using a list of `Selector`s to define what it needs, a `Task` would defer the "querying" to an `@rule` that computes a particular type (which might be bespoke for that `Task`).

aiohttp server shifts keepalive closing time on every incoming request.

My test server and client are here: https://gist.github.com/asvetlov/0a4e4c40bca14ff797f6e27cfef4220c

Explanation of my understanding:

If timeout is expired the connection is ultimately closed, and this is perfectly fine: the connection is in idle state, no response to send connection closing flag.
Waiting for a new incoming request after keepalive timeout expiration violates the whole keepalive idea: disconnection may appear much later or not appear at all.

I think it makes sense for marshmallow to support this use case. We should explore marshmallow's existing functionality before we add a feature to the core.

Your use case:

- Deserializing XML
- Deserializing an empty string to `None`
- Enabling special `None` handling per `Field` instance

The general solution for customizing deserialization behavior should be a custom field.

http://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/custom_fields.html

```py
from marshmallow import fields

class CustomField(fields.String):
    def _deserialize(self, value, attr, obj):
        if value == '':
            return None
        return super(CustomField, self)._deserialize(value, attr, obj)
```

Is there anything about this that doesn't work for your use case?

@russellb For the new segment it should be `status_chars['rebase:interactive']`. It is better if branch segment shows `{branch}*` (`*` *may* be a separate subsegment, but emitted by branch segment; not necessary). Do not forget that `branch_name_from_config_file` is supposed to be called *only* when `HEAD` file changes (tracked by inotify).

New VCS segment should not be VCS-specific. But it is not necessary to provide any implementation, *except* the one returning `None` at the end for other VCS types if you don’t know how to get such statuses.

Note about `status_chars['rebase:interactive']`: ` (REBASE-i)` is rather lengthy, but alternatives may be less readable. So actual string needs to be configurable so that it is possible to replace them with unicode characters or less lengthy strings. `:` as a separator is not necessary, but this is a convention for highlight groups, and I expect such segment to return something like

    status_components = status.split(':')
    return [{
        'contents': status_chars[status],
        'highlight_groups': ['dirstatus:' + ':'.join(status_components[:i]) for i in range(len(status_components), 0, -1)] + ['dirstatus']
    }]
(i.e. so that for `rebase:interactive` it will request `['dirstatus:rebase:interactive', 'dirstatus:rebase', 'dirstatus']` highlight groups). Different highlight groups are needed to put more visual pressure on such statuses if needed because they require resolution.

---

About `powerline.lib.path.join`: this function exists because some functions may receive *both* `b'foo/bar'` and `u'foo/bar'` paths (i.e. both bytes and unicode) and it is easier to write such function then to use only one kind of paths, especially given that I must not assume filesystem using specific encoding.

The `--reverse` flag is only for use with the `--disk-space` flag:

```
  --reverse BOOLEAN   Only valid with --disk-space. Affects sort order of the
                      indices.  True means reverse-alphabetical (if dates are
                      involved, older is deleted first).  [default: True]
```

You're indicating that you want to delete older than 3 months, but are providing a _daily_ timestamp.  The `--time-unit` is not merely a unit of time, but should be the unit of time by which your indices are reckoned.  As a result, you should convert months to days, then delete older than 90 days instead:

```
curator delete indices --older-than 90 --time-unit days --timestring %Y.%m.%d --prefix "logstash-"
```

The general proposition of using `~=` (or even `^=`, which does not exist in pip) is still valid, and I have seen this raised quite a few times now. `*` means every re-lock and update could potentially break user code because a new major version is released and it is not compatible with the application. 

You could argue the user should be aware of this, and use e.g. `django~=2.1.0` in the first place. But still, there is no good way to say “I want the latest ~= possible” instead of manually looking it up. It would be immensely helpful to either a) set this as default, and require `django==*` to explicitly get `*`, or b) have a command flag as mentioned above (an option in Pipfile less so IMO).

While the problem described is probably fixed, the user experience side of this proposition is still worth discussing.

I've added code to passthrough the `replace` argument from `.add` to callbacks, which fixes the setup_requires test but I'm not sure it's the right thing to do. It depends, to some degree, on what `.activate()` is meant to do, and when it is meant to be called. In master, it's called for every distribution found on sys.path and added with `dist.add()`, and its behavior is to ensure that every package added has a high import priority. The first commit on this PR is to call it on the same packages, but never modify import priority if packages are found.

Another behavior would be to only activate 'new' packages with high priority, and leave those found by default as lower priority

You're right - the file finders functionality can be surprising, especially because the behavior is injected by the presence of third-party libs.

I do recommend that these file finder libraries only be installed transiently, as part of a build of a single package. Once PEP 518 lands, it should better guarantee that dependencies like these aren't brought into the build of your package by the build of other packages in the same session.

But as far as deprecating and removing this functionality, I don't think that can happen. It provides too much value and has too many users relying on it. The advantages of the feature outweigh the harm of issues like these.

I don't find that in my environment that these file finders libraries get installed permanently. I suggest you track down what is causing these packages to be installed and see if you can convince them not to install those packages.

Hope that helps.

If the named suite is a module, and the module has an additional_tests() function, it is called and the results are added to the tests to be run. If the named suite is a package, any submodules and subpackages are recursively added to the overall test suite.

>     Specifying this argument enables use of the test command to run the specified test suite, e.g. via setup.py test. See the section on the test command below for more details.

There is a solution if you use the newer `component` API; both asyncio and twisted versions of this do auto-reconnect (with backoff and jitter etc). See `examples/{asyncio,twisted}/wamp/component/*` for some examples.

We've been using a higher-level thing than both of those for WAMP -- but then again, it's a higher-level thing anyway (e.g. https://autobahn.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/autobahn.twisted.html#component).

We don't have anything like the above (besides the existing Twisted endpoints) for WebSocket. Another piece here is the desire to have a similar (or at least consistent) API between Twisted and asyncio (but since asyncio lacks endpoints, we've departed already somewhat). Adding "a better way" to create WebSocket endpoints does make sense, though -- ``IAgentEndpointFactory`` looks like good inspiration here, thanks!

Thanks for the quick reply.
My argument is, that the WebSocket message, that was received is not invalid:
To my understanding, WebSockets should be closed by a closing frame on both sides (https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6455#section-1.4).
Lines 714-738 in `protocol.py` handle the situation, where the side running the code first sends the closing and then receives the closing frame from other side. This is also, what the comment says:
```python
# We already initiated the closing handshake, so this
# is the peer's reply to our close frame.
```
So the closing frame from the other side is not invalid and the underlying connection should be teared down gracefully.

No, it doesn't work this way. Patroni on the replica will never make a decision to promote while the leader lock is present. Patroni on the master that is shutting down will not relinquish this lock until it makes sure the master is not running (by checking its PID among other things). 

BTW, the 3rd step is actually not required in most cases, since the former master would start up as a replica and rejoin the new master (in case there are some issues we could use pg_rewind in order to remove the discrepancy).

> BACKUP_NUM_TO_RETAIN = 2 - is backups for the past 2 days? Old backups are to be deleted from bucket, right?

Yes.

> BACKUP_SCHEDULE = "00 01 * * *" - can I do each 10 mins or so? How critical is it?

You can, but it doesn't make much sense.  It's possible restore to any point in time even with using the most oldest backup.  You definitely need to learn about PITR: https://www.postgresql.org/docs/10/static/continuous-archiving.html

This is normal: the replica registers itself in the DCS and tries to connect to a given replication slot on the master. It fails for a few times initially, but once the master notices the new member and creates the slot - the error disappears.

An obvious enhancement to this would be to create the slot by the replica itself by connecting to the master with the replication protocol. The master, then, will only be responsible for removing obsolete replication slots once the corresponding members disappear from DCS.

So, I notice that the `tag` attribute from `tifffile` is directly given out.
Wouldn't it be better to curate the list of metadata that's being shown ? This way it can be standardized for other plugins too. Otherwise, the user will have to read the doumentation of each backend individually and also decipher which backend was used in the file to be able to use the metadata (as otherwise the keys will change).

The metadata that is exposed by the function can be added based on requirement, otherwise `skimage` which s an image processing toolkit would be bogged down by exif/meta data reading.

Also, the tiff tags mentioned at [tiff tags](http://www.digitalpreservation.gov/formats/content/tiff_tags.shtml) have: Comments, Model (The model of the scanner), Artist, TargetPrinter, XMP, BatteryLevel, Copyright, ICCProfile, GPSInfo, and a whole lot of information that an image processing toolkit wouldn't care about. This seems like unnecessary noise. To read that sort of meta-information, the user should actually use an exif/meta reader like exiftool, gexiv2, etc.

Hi @BCsRock , thanks for the report.

> this could be a bug in case an account joins a payment channel with zero Ethereum or having not enough funds to pay the update / settlement transactions. It seems to run into a stucked state with a repeatedly failing transaction.

In the latest release (v0.5.0 - Tanacetum) we added two strategies to mitigate this problem:

- The user won't be able to open a channel if he doesn't have enough ether to complete the channel lifecycle. This, however, doesn't fix the case where a node `acc1` which has ether opens a channel with a node `acc2` which doesn't have ether.
- We added a warning to inform the user when the account's ether is insufficient, (issue #1998 , implemented by #2031), so the node `acc2` would have a notification printed to the console when `acc1` opens a channel with him.

This is not a perfect strategy and will be improved, for more information refer to the issue #1861

> Restarting Raiden client with Acc2 tries to make Update Nonclosing balance proof transaction transaction, but fails with error message below:

Prior to v0.5.0 we had to retry all transactions on every restart (not necessarily all where re-sent, but that was the case for update transfer), as @LefterisJP mentioned this was fixed (issue #1950) which is integrated since v0.5.0. So, on the latest version transaction known to fail would not be sent.

Details about the error:

- [the channel was opened](https://ropsten.etherscan.io/tx/0x28c2fb442a5b9afd4140cd8b8aade4869eb435581f03cbe04fb32d6bbaae2f24#eventlog) with a settlement window of 501 blocks.
- [the channel was closed](https://ropsten.etherscan.io/tx/0x169247734eeaacf7359be9e550df40633e04e87deacd997c8e374ce8a6b77a96#eventlog) at block 3791424
- The failing transaction linked was at the block height 3801071, which is 9146 blocks after the settlement window, that is why the resending the transaction failed, even thought acc2 had ether.

With the above said we have to do two things in order to fix this (as of v0.5.0):

- The state machine assumes the partner will always successfully call update transfer, and includes both [balance proofs in the settlement call](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/master/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L1678-L1683), if the update transaction fails then the settle transaction will also fail. To fix this the settle state change should not include the balance proof, but the balance hash, and the balance proof should be recovered from the WAL.
- The transaction queue is cleared exclusively based on the available events, in this case the settle event is necessary to clear the update transaction from the queue, but the settle event would never happen because of the above bug, so we should add the block number and clear time dependent transactions which have lost the time frame to successfully execute.

> Was just looking at that code. But what should the action on the channel be if updateTransfer() was called?

The state of the channel should be transition from "need to call update" to "dont need to call update" iff the state change contains the latest balance proof hash. And the contract send update should not be sent if the current state is "dont need to call update"

Edit: I was in the middle of writing this up when @hzpc-joostk made his latest comment.  There's probably more detail than necessary here, but I'm going to go ahead and post it anyway, and probably come back again in the future to use for other situations.

---

Let me explain for you guys what's happening in this part of the code--give you some background and context--and then maybe we can figure it out together.

Probably should start by focusing on what's happening in https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.9/conda/core/path_actions.py#L389-L425

```python
def verify(self):
    validation_error = super(PrefixReplaceLinkAction, self).verify()
    if validation_error:
        return validation_error

    if islink(self.source_full_path):
        log.trace("ignoring prefix update for symlink with source path %s",
                  self.source_full_path)
        # return
        assert False, "I don't think this is the right place to ignore this"

    self.intermediate_path = join(self.transaction_context['temp_dir'], text_type(uuid4()))

    log.trace("copying %s => %s", self.source_full_path, self.intermediate_path)
    create_link(self.source_full_path, self.intermediate_path, LinkType.copy)

    try:
        log.trace("rewriting prefixes in %s", self.target_full_path)
        update_prefix(self.intermediate_path,
                      context.target_prefix_override or self.target_prefix,
                      self.prefix_placeholder,
                      self.file_mode)
    except _PaddingError:
        raise PaddingError(self.target_full_path, self.prefix_placeholder,
                           len(self.prefix_placeholder))

    sha256_in_prefix = compute_sha256sum(self.intermediate_path)

    self.prefix_path_data = PathDataV1.from_objects(
        self.prefix_path_data,
        file_mode=self.file_mode,
        path_type=PathType.hardlink,
        prefix_placeholder=self.prefix_placeholder,
        sha256_in_prefix=sha256_in_prefix,
    )
```

When we get here, conda's solver has already ran to decide what needs to be uninstalled and installed into an environment, and all the packages have been downloaded and extracted into the package cache.  The uninstall-install process is done in what we call an [`UnlinkLinkTransaction`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.9/conda/core/link.py#L142), which has three stages: (1) prepare, (2) verify, (3) execute.  During the prepare stage, for *every single* path we're going to modify, we instantiate some flavor of [`PathAction`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.9/conda/core/path_actions.py#L59), which itself has four required methods: (1) verify, (2) execute, (3) reverse, (4) cleanup.  So in theory, we should have full knowledge of every disk mutation we're going to make after we're done with the prepare stage.

During the verify stage, we do verification of certain things at the [whole transaction level](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.9/conda/core/link.py#L400) and at the [per-prefix level](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.9/conda/core/link.py#L330) (transactions are designed with the capability to be multi-prefix transactions, for the anticipated future feature addition of [private application environments](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3912)), and then we also call the `verify()` method for every `PathAction` we've instantiated during the prepare phase.  More specifics on this last part in a bit, because that's where we're getting tripped up.

After the verify stage is done, we actually *begin* the transaction, where we start to call the `execute()` method for every `PathAction`.  If we get through them all without an unexpected exception happening, the transaction has completed successfully, and we finish by calling the `cleanup()` method for every `PathAction`.  However, if anything unexpected happens during the transaction, we roll back the transaction by calling `reverse()` on every `PathAction` that had been executed so far.

Okay back to details of the `verify()` step for individual `PathAction`s.  By far the most used is `LinkPathAction`, and the [`verify()`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.4.9/conda/core/path_actions.py#L257-L358) method there.  `LinkPathAction` takes a specific path in the package cache, and correctly links it into the target prefix.  Depending on configuration, the type of the path being linked, etc., we might be hard-linking, soft-linking, copying, just creating a directory, creating a new relative soft link, etc.  That's all taken care of by `LinkPathAction`.  The `verify()` method makes sure the path we're going to be linking *actually exists* in the package cache, and makes sure it hasn't been modified from what's expected by checking the sha256.

There's a variant of `LinkPathAction` that we also use a lot, `PrefixReplaceLinkAction`, which we use when we'd ideally like to just hard-link the path into the prefix, but instead we have to copy it, because we also have to do prefix rewrites within the content of the file.  Here during the `verify()` step, we're copying the content of the path in the package cache to a temporary location on disk, and doing the prefix rewriting on the file in that temporary location.  The `execute()` method then uses the content of that temporary location to link into the prefix, and `cleanup()` removes that temporary location.

Write permissions of this temporary location is where we're having problem here.

Yes, channels are listed (in `~/.condarc`) and shown (by various `conda list` and `conda info` commands) in priority order. The workings of the solver are pretty complicated but when all else is equal, channel priority kicks in.

Since all of your packages are available on defaults, those channels are not adding anything for you, and indeed are causing your binary incompatibility.

If there are packages that you need that `defaults` does not provide, you will need to build them yourself to get the backwards compatibility with `glibc 2.5` that you need.  Continuum offers package building services if you'd like: sales@continuum.io.

Some updates!
I've been working non-stop on keep improving the performance with the `Query Builder`.

# Benchmarks

## Retrieving 10k ObjectTypes
Doing something similar to the following query where `allContainers` type is a `[ObjectType]` and `x` is a `Integer`:
```graphql
{
  allContainers {
    x
  }
}
```
  
  - With Query Builder: https://travis-ci.org/graphql-python/graphql-core/jobs/158369656#L488
> 30ms

  - Without Query Builder: https://travis-ci.org/graphql-python/graphql-core/jobs/158369656#L484
> 350ms


## Retrieving 10k items in a List
Doing something similar to the following query where `allInts` type is a `[Integer]`
```graphql
{
  allInts
}
```
  
  - With Query Builder: https://travis-ci.org/graphql-python/graphql-core/jobs/158369656#L483
> 12ms

  - Without Query Builder: https://travis-ci.org/graphql-python/graphql-core/jobs/158369656#L486
> 30ms

# Conclusion

The work I'm doing so far is being a demonstration the code performance still have margins to improve while preserving fully compatibility with GraphQL syntax.

The proof of concept speedup goes between 3x and 10x while maintaining the syntax and features `GraphQL` have. Still a lot of work to do there, but it's a first approach that let us discover new paths for speed improvement.


# Extra

I think by using `Cython` for some critical instructions we can gain about another 10-20x in speed.


# Transport

Apart of using Cython I'm thinking how we can plug multiple kind of transports into GraphQL.
So instead of creating Python Objects each time we are accessing a field, and then transforming the result to `JSON`, another approach could be transform the values directly into `JSON` or whatever transport we are using.

This way the result could be created directly in the output format. This way we can plug other transports like `binary` (CapN Proto/FlatBuffers/Thrift/others), `msgpack` or any other thing we could think of.

> But for now, the show must go on!

Agreed! I also like the idea of it being under the umbrella of an organization, hopefully more Python GraphQL libraries/projects will join in, too!

Multiple outputs can be useful in the case you have some computation with multiple results where a lot of the work is shared. For example I have a layer that returns a bunch of rank metrics (accuracy@1, accuracy@2, accuracy@3 etc). Currently that means I have to split off the intermediate computation into a separate layer in order to reuse it, then create a separate layer from that for each metric. Not the end of the world but in some situations you can end up chaining together lots of tiny little custom layers and it makes you wonder if you'd've been better off just using plain theano.

SOPS uses the file's extension to deduce the format it should use (YAML, JSON or binary). Since in this case the file doesn't have an extension, you need to use the `--input-type` and `--output-type` flags, like so:

```bash
❯ cat test.json |  sops --input-type json --output-type json -d /dev/stdin 
{
	"hello": "world"
}
```

Cowboy-style fix is patching python's enum module. 
Checked on 2.7.x, but should be OK with 3.x

```python
import enum

def _is_sunder_fix(name):
    """Returns True if a _sunder_ name, False otherwise.
    Patched: doesn't crash on empty strings
    """
    return (name[:1] == name[-1:] == '_' and
            name[1:2] != '_' and
            name[-2:-1] != '_' and
            len(name) > 2)

enum._is_sunder = _is_sunder_fix
```

@codinguncut ,
Although it's not documented (nor explicitly tested), 2 of the extractors already support passing an lxml document directly (e.g. result of an lxml parser's `.fromstring()`, which is how it's implemented for `.extract()`):
* [`extruct.jsonld.JsonLdExtractor.extract_items(document)`](https://github.com/scrapinghub/extruct/blob/5f58236024530004f5cb824444aa04ef6f9db19f/extruct/jsonld.py#L20)
* [`w3cmicrodata.LxmlMicrodataExtractor.extract_items(document, url)`](https://github.com/scrapinghub/extruct/blob/5f58236024530004f5cb824444aa04ef6f9db19f/extruct/w3cmicrodata.py#L47)

The RDFa extractor is a bit different since rdflib is tricked into thinking it is handling an `xml.dom` tree, but the lxml parser is available: [`extruct.rdfa.XmlDomHTMLParser`](https://github.com/scrapinghub/extruct/blob/b9dc15c23bc81bb7cda24b4095d2cab9682a0f75/extruct/rdfa.py#L170) and a method can be added to pass an `xml.dom` compatible tree.

Waiting on events on a single zmq socket in multiple greenlets is not supported. If it were supported in terms of there not being an error, you would still have a race as to who gets to handle the events, and the first event listener could change the state so that other event listeners that wake on the same event would get no events to process. A semaphore works because as long as you don't have multiple greenlets *waiting on events* on the same socket at the same time, you are fine.

EAGAIN means that you are trying to send messages faster than zmq can handle. This is triggered by your setting SNDTIMEO to a finite value.  If it cannot send within that time limit, this error is raised. If you hadn't set SNDTIMEO, then the send call would block until it can be sent. It looks like your code is trying to replicate the default behavior, so maybe setting SNDTIMEO isn't what you want.

That's probably our initial sin on naming the endpoints. The name in the object comes directly from the model and it's singular. I could hack it and turn into plural in the serializer, but I prefer to rename endpoints to singular if that is preferred. This will be a pretty major change, so needs to be announced with some longer lead time.

The only way I can think of would be to allow a non-global context to
"borrow" threads from the global pool, and only create it's own if there
are none available in the global pool. Unfortunately my C skills are not up
to much, and I'm not sure how that would even work in a multithreaded
environment with multiple contextual calls being made concurrently.

FWIW I think it's a reasonable option to make the bcolz default to use the
global blosc calls, so nothing will change for existing bcolz users working
in non-threaded environments. Then we add a switch
(bcolz.blosc_use_context()) so multi-threaded programs can put bcolz into
contextual mode. When in contextual mode, bcolz only ever requests a single
blosc internal thread. This gives very good performance with dask and
avoids any hierarchies of threads.
