I've used ls since very early on in the project's life and I'm also an avid yt-dl user of many years (for downloads & streaming), one thing that people don't seem to mention when making comparisons is how they handle caching. Not only is ls superior in how it handles stream caches (greatly reducing drops as a result) but also in my experience yt-dl requires more overhead in terms of bandwidth and cpu than ls does. 

yt-dl isn't designed for streaming and that's what this project is about.

I'd say that we should see if we can use a free service of a real hosting provider before going the route of using a private server.

On Bintray you can get 1TB/m of bandwidth and 10GB/m of storage for free in their open-source plan.
https://bintray.com/account/pricing?tab=account&type=pricing
This should be enough for now and can be easily set up in travis. I have tested this a couple of times in the past months and it worked quite well. I was using it for custom DEB and RPM repos though and custom code was required for signing the repos (due to a bug of Travis' deploy module), but this isn't required here, since we'd only upload binaries for Windows anyway.

If this isn't enough and paid servers/services are required, I'd recommend the dedicated servers of OVH, which all have unlimited bandwidth and start at 5€. You can even get a dedicated server for a couple of bucks (https://www.kimsufi.com/en/). I'm using dedicated OVH servers since 2008 and it's a much better idea than S3, at a fixed cost.

And as another alternative, we could still use a separate repo on github and upload nightlies by using Github's release API, like I've mentioned in the other thread a couple of days ago.

Btw, I like the idea of building real nightlies. This saves storage space, computation time in the CI env and is a better scheme overall. Even if it comes at the cost of not being able to let people test newly committed code, those people can still use git, pip or other methods instead, which should be okay for those who are interested in "real-time development" here.

Actually, I've been experimenting on the side on my current fork and believe I have the correct solution. It's on the typehint-acme branch on my fork. Since mypy must be executed under Python 3, and since we're using the comment style annotations, then the solution is to add the following to the top of modules being type-hinted:

MYPY = False
if MYPY:
    from typing import List, Any, <etc....>  # pylint: disable=unused-import
    from functools import partial  

When mypy executes, it will override the MYPY = False and execute the import statements guarded by it. However this will not impact the code builds even under Python 2.6. Just need to remember to add the pylint override on the protected imports so it doesn't flag these imports as errors.

Also in the case where the type-hinted code has a pylint override, the type-hint comment comes first.  You can look at my fork for examples.

I looked into how the `__init__`s are picked up before. The reason we need to put the same thing in every `vsc/__init__.py` (or `easybuild/__init__.py`) are exactly because only one is actually executed (the first one that is found in the Python search path).

The magic in `easybuild/__init__.py` is wrong in the sense that it's not zip-safe yet, that's true. We'll fix that.

Note that the warnings w.r.t. the `easybuild` namespace are triggered via `vsc/__init__.py` which imports `pkg_resources`, see the error messages mentioned in the description in https://github.com/hpcugent/easybuild-framework/issues/1588.

Using gluon I find that MXNet allocates 1.92GB in memory when loading the model.
When running one image through the network, memory peak at 2.10GB and then go down to 2.02GB

Using the Module API, it allocates 1.4GB

```python
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gluon

# load model from model zoo
net = gluon.model_zoo.vision.vgg16(pretrained=True, ctx=mx.gpu()) #1.9GB
net(mx.nd.ones((1,3,224,224), mx.gpu())) #2.1GB

# export the parameters
net.hybridize()
net(mx.nd.ones((1,3,224,224), mx.gpu()))
net.export('vgg16')

# Load in symbol
sym, arg_params, aux_params = mx.model.load_checkpoint('vgg16', 0)
mod = mx.mod.Module(symbol=sym, context=mx.gpu(0), label_names=None)
mod.bind(for_training=False, data_shapes=[('data', (1,3,224,224))])
mod.set_params(arg_params, aux_params) # 1.4 GB
```
